← the Atlas

sutra — the terminal-workflow substrate

seedling·proposed·JUN 30, 2026

sutra (the connecting thread) is the receptacle the remote-terminals plan was missing — but scoped to ONE host at a time. You CONNECT it to a host (local or an ssh remote); it dials that host's kaval (PTY) and pulam (awareness + fs/git), and owns the terminal lifecycle over that connection — create · attach · kill · sleep/wake · reconnect · boot re-adopt — behind sealed id-keyed dispatch (private endpoints, so "hard-pin local" is a COMPILE ERROR). Host is CONNECTION CONTEXT, not a per-terminal property: you switch hosts with a dropdown and only that host's terminals show, so there is NO composite key and NO HostLocation threaded through every op — a bare ptyId is the whole id. The cross-host "which agent needs me everywhere" glance is pulam-web's job, not kolu's. Both UIs EMBED sutra (kolu-server for the web client, kolu-tui) with the dependency arrow pointing OUT to kaval, pulam and surface-nix-host. About half of kolu-server migrates in; kolu keeps the durable cross-host session + its own view chrome.

Contents

  • What it owns — and why it’s electricity, not the server’s internals renamed
  • One host at a time — the decision that deletes the composite key
  • The API both embedders call
  • Slimming kolu-server — and the session split
  • The extraction phase
  • See also

@kolu/sutra — the receptacle the finale was missing. sutra (Sanskrit: the connecting thread) is the thread that binds you to one host’s terminals — the sibling of kaval, pulam and drishti in kolu’s graduated-electricity family. Every hard thing left in remote terminals — dialing a host, routing a lifecycle op to its kaval/pulam, reconnecting an ssh link, re-adopting surviving PTYs at boot — is one volatility: the terminal workflow on a host you may be reaching over ssh. Today it’s not a boundary, it’s a convention smeared across kolu-server: the local endpoint is exported and directly reachable, so “hard-pin local / bypass the host router” is a writable expression a missed seam forgets (the round-2 type-enforcement debate verified the smear; debates/pr1637/). This note lifts that volatility into one @kolu/* package both UIs embed — and, crucially, scopes it to one host at a time, which is what turns ssh-remote from a cross-cutting rewrite into a connection you dial.

Why this note exists — naming the receptacle, not the organ

A live debate proposed graduating a kolu-TUI as the electricity. That names an organ: a TUI is a product surface (panes, keybindings, attach) — it fails electricity test ① (domain-agnostic) on sight, exactly the “history stack vs. browser” trap. The electricity is the substrate the TUI would consume. The TUI’s role is the other half of the proof: it’s the second consumer that shows the substrate graduated — the way drishti proved @kolu/surface, kaval-tui proved kaval’s ptyHostSurface, and pulam-tui proved pulam’s awareness surface. Decided across debates/pr1637/ + debates/sutra-reconsidered/; the substrate-not-TUI call and the single-host scoping both came out of that 4-agent record.

What it owns — and why it’s electricity, not the server’s internals renamed

Run the three electricity tests honestly:

  • ① Domain-agnostic? sutra’s interface is the terminal workflow on a host, shared verbatim by a browser GUI and a TUI — no Solid, no canvas, no Code-tab, no xterm leaks across it. The thing that fails ① is the TUI, not this.
  • ② A real volatility? Yes, and a different one than the pieces already graduated. kaval owns PTY transport; pulam owns awareness + fs/git observation; @kolu/surface-nix-host owns the ssh/Nix dial. None owns kolu’s terminal lifecycle over a (possibly remote) connection — create/attach/kill/sleep/wake/adopt, dialing the host’s kaval + pulam, surviving a dropped link, re-adopting at boot. That orchestration is the smear the round-2 debate found — a transport-grade volatility (reconnect, adoption, dial), not a bounded algorithm.
  • ③ Graduates, second consumer plugs in? It ships as @kolu/sutra and kolu-tui embeds it (the next note) — the next work on the roadmap.

The bar this must clear — or it's just a rename

The substrate must encapsulate a genuine volatility — the lifecycle/dial/reconnect/adoption orchestration — not “kolu-server internals given a package name.” Two falsifiable conditions hold it there: (1) kaval-tui already proves the raw PTY transport over ssh, so sutra’s value is explicitly the kolu orchestration both consumers share, never the transport (that’s kaval’s); (2) kolu-tui must EMBED it and dial the host itself — if the TUI were a thin renderer of a running kolu-server, nothing graduates and it’s a second UI, not a second consumer. Both are load-bearing in the phase plan.

What it owns (per connection — one host):

Capability What it encapsulates Grounded in today’s code
Sealed id-keyed dispatch per-terminal ops as op(id) that resolve the connected host’s endpoint internally; the endpoint instance + resolver + *Local* helpers are private, so no op can hard-pin the local endpoint when the connection is remote seals the resolver-bypass at packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/resolve.ts:39-53 + the 9 *Local* escape hatches at packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/local.ts:1143-1283
Dial + reconnect + adoption connect to the host’s kaval + pulam over ssh, survive a dropped link, re-dial-re-adopt the host’s surviving PTYs at boot adoptSurvivingSession packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/reattach.ts:61-174 + the pure reconcile packages/server/src/reconcile.ts:68-96; getHostSession backoff + durable kaval --stdio
Host-local session / restore which terminals exist on this host, which are sleeping, their restore targets — the live registry for the connection the registry Map<PtyId, …> at packages/server/src/terminal-registry.ts:70 (bare id — no rekey)

What it consumes (deps point out): kaval (PTY, remote over ssh), pulam (awareness + fs/git, dialed as one surface), @kolu/surface-nix-host (getHostSession — the ssh/Nix dial), @kolu/surface (the mirror). sutra depends on these; neither UI is depended on by sutra.

One host at a time — the decision that deletes the composite key

The pivotal call, settled by a 4-agent debate (debates/sutra-reconsidered/): a kolu surface shows ONE host’s terminals at a time. Host is connection context — you pick it with a dropdown (kinda like tmux sessions), and switching hosts tears down one sutra connection and opens another. There is no surface that mixes local and remote tiles.

That one decision is the biggest honest simplification in the whole plan, because it makes host stop being a per-terminal property:

  • No composite TerminalKey. Within a single-host connection, a terminal’s id is just its bare kaval ptyId (z.string().uuid(), packages/terminal-workspace/src/schema.ts:48). Two hosts’ PTYs never share a surface, so they can’t collide — the cross-host aggregation key (R9.3’s HostTerminalKey) simply isn’t needed.
  • No HostLocation threaded through every op. The host lives at the connection boundary (dialed once), not on each create/attach/sleep call. resolveTerminalEndpoint(entry.meta.location) per terminal collapses to “the connection’s endpoint.”
  • No cross-host registry / forEachHost / branded complete-host sweep. There is one host per connection; boot adoption is that host’s reconcile, not a sweep over a map. The round-1 “save after adopting one host of three” bug can’t be written because there is no multi-host save.

What's lost, and why it's fine — the fleet glance is pulam-web's job

The cost is real: you can’t put a local tile and a nix@prod tile side by side in one canvas, and the Dock rail shows one host at a time. But the thing that loss would hurt — “which agent needs me, across ALL my hosts, at once” — is already built, in the right place: pulam-web is the browser fleet board, spanning every host, sorted needs-you-first, and it does multi-host as N single-host connections with host identity living only at the transport layer (verified across the 4-agent debate). pulam-tui is likewise single-daemon and defers the fleet view to pulam-web. So kolu is the workbench for the selected host (tmux-style); the cross-host glance is pulam-web’s. Nothing kolu actually does — a terminal, its Code tab, its awareness, its restore — is cross-host; it’s all intra-host. The composite key was paying for a requirement kolu didn’t have.

The API both embedders call

You connect sutra to a host, then drive terminals by bare id. The endpoint, the resolver and the local impls are package-private, so no op can reach around the connection to a hardcoded local endpoint.

// @kolu/sutra — a SINGLE-HOST terminal-workflow surface. Connect it to ONE host; everything
// below is scoped to that connection. Switching hosts = connect a fresh one.
// PRIVATE (not exported): TerminalEndpoint, resolveTerminalEndpoint, the LocalTerminalEndpoint
// instance, every *Local* helper — so "hard-pin the local endpoint" has no symbol to write.

function connectSutra(host: HostLocation): TerminalSubstrate;   // dials host's kaval + pulam

export interface TerminalSubstrate {
  // —— per-terminal ops: BARE ptyId; the connected host's endpoint resolves INTERNALLY ——
  create(opts: PtySpawnOpts): TerminalInfo;                 // spawns on the connected host
  attach(id: PtyId, signal?: AbortSignal): Promise<TerminalAttachment>;
  sendInput(id: PtyId, data: string): void;
  resize(id: PtyId, cols: number, rows: number): void;
  screenText(id: PtyId, extent?: ScreenExtent): Promise<string>;
  kill(id: PtyId): Promise<TerminalInfo | undefined>;
  sleep(id: PtyId): Promise<void>;         // was beginSleepLocal + saveSession + releaseSleptLocalPty
  wake(id: PtyId): TerminalInfo | undefined;
  discardSleeping(id: PtyId): boolean;
  restoreSleeping(record: SavedSleepingTerminal): boolean;

  // —— awareness: one framed stream per terminal (folded by the consumer) ——
  subscribeTerminal(id: PtyId): AsyncIterable<TerminalFrame>;

  // —— connection-scoped session ops (this host only — no cross-host sweep) ——
  killAll(): Promise<void>;                 // the connected host's terminals
  adoptOnConnect(): Promise<void>;          // re-dial-re-adopt this host's survivors at boot
}

(PtyId is the bare kaval uuid. Types like TerminalFrame / ScreenExtent are illustrative — an implementer derives them from the existing TerminalEndpoint + awareness code; the shape that matters is the sealing + the bare id.)

This is not new surface area — it’s today’s call sites with the escape hatches removed and the host lifted to the connection. Every op already exists; it’s just reached three incompatible ways the facade collapses into one:

Op How it routes today Through sutra
attach · kill the resolver, per-terminal — resolveTerminalEndpoint(entry.meta.location).attach(…) packages/server/src/router.ts:183-186 op(id) — the connection’s endpoint, resolved once
resize · sendInput · screenText a raw registry lookup + handle verb — getActiveTerminal(id)?.handle.write(…) packages/server/src/router.ts:118-125 op(id) — the registry lookup moves inside
sleep · wake · discardSleeping · restoreSleeping direct *Local* calls that bypass the resolver entirely — wakeLocalTerminal(id) packages/server/src/router.ts:242-253 op(id) — the bypass becomes unspellable
create crypto.randomUUID() then a constant-pinned localEndpoint = resolveTerminalEndpoint(LOCAL_LOCATION) packages/server/src/terminals.ts:49-117 create(opts) — spawns on the connected host, no location param

The guarantee: a missed host-seam is a COMPILE ERROR, not a code-review catch

Because TerminalEndpoint, resolveTerminalEndpoint, the localTerminalEndpoint instance and all nine *Local* helpers become package-private, the offending expressions have no symbols to write:

  • discardLocalSleeping(id) → Cannot find name 'discardLocalSleeping'
  • localEndpoint.killAllTerminals() → Cannot find name 'localEndpoint'
  • resolveTerminalEndpoint(LOCAL_LOCATION).sleep(id) → Cannot find name 'resolveTerminalEndpoint'

Strictly stronger than a branded “routed” type: a brand rejects a value that still exists; removing the producer means there’s nothing to form. The compiler does the seam-hunt that the manual lifecycle-threading prep did by hand — which is why that prep dissolves into this one. Single-host makes the guarantee smaller: sutra seals one endpoint (the connected host’s), not a map of them.

Both embedders call this identically — connect a host, then drive by bare id:

// kolu-server — the web client's oRPC handlers (packages/server):
const sutra = connectSutra(selectedHost);   // "local" by default; the dropdown switches it
await sutra.attach(id, signal);
sutra.create(opts);

// kolu-tui — the Dock's attach action + create command (the SAME package, embedded):
const sutra = connectSutra(selectedHost);   // kolu-tui dials its own host
await sutra.attach(id, signal);             // byte-for-byte the same call
sutra.create(opts);

sutra doesn’t know or care which embedder it’s inside, or whether its host is local or an ssh remote — that ignorance is test ① passing.

Slimming kolu-server — and the session split

The extraction is the move that makes remote terminals small. Today the lifecycle volatility is ~half of kolu-server’s source, threaded by hand at every site. Lift it into sutra and the remote host is just a different connectSutra argument — a dial, not a cross-cutting edit.

Migrates into @kolu/sutra (the volatility) LOC Stays in kolu-server (web/HTTP/product) LOC
terminalEndpoint/* — the lifecycle engine, sleep/wake, adopt, the resolver 1981 index.ts — the Hono HTTP server, oRPC handlers, static serving, TLS 500
ptyHost/* — daemon supervision, dial, handshake, restart policy 670 state.ts — on-disk persisted-state + migrations 539
terminal-registry.ts · terminals.ts (lifecycle verbs) · reconcile.ts 595 surface.ts · router.ts — the surface/contract serving + oRPC glue 838
iframePreviewRoute.ts, pwaIdentity.ts, tls.ts, … web-only rest

That’s ~3246 LOC ≈ 52% of kolu-server’s non-test src/ (a hair less once terminals.ts’s client-chrome setters — netted out below — stay behind), plus its matching tests (~1491 LOC).

Q1 — who owns session/restore: sutra owns host-local, kolu keeps the cross-host durable save

The current saved session is braided — terminal-restore facts and web/UI chrome live together (packages/common/src/surface.ts:313-335 for the authored server fields; client fields like canvasLayout/rightPanel/intent alongside). Unbraid it along the connection line:

  • sutra owns the host-local restore — the connected host’s live registry, sleeping records, restore targets, adoption. This is memoryless-producer + snapshot-half fold; the two remembered facts (recency, launch line) stay on kolu.authored (terminalEndpoint/metadata.ts splits: commit-to-registry is sutra, publish-to-kolu-surface is kolu). kolu still owns memory.
  • kolu keeps the durable CROSS-host session — which hosts the user had open and which terminals on each, so a kolu restart re-connectSutras each saved host and re-adopts. That list spans hosts, so it’s kolu’s, not any one sutra’s. session.ts / state.ts stay in kolu.
  • Each UI keeps its own view chrome — the web canvas layout is kolu-server’s; the TUI has no canvas.

So the boundary the whole awareness design rests on is unchanged: the producer is memoryless, kolu remembers.

The extraction phase

One PR: extract @kolu/sutra and seal the dispatch. A pure local-preserving refactor + a package move — every terminal is on the local connection today, so every op(id) resolves to the same local endpoint and create/attach/kill/sleep/wake/adoption stay byte-identical. It absorbs the old manual lifecycle-threading prep (there is no seam-hunt — the missed seam is a compile error) and it is the foundation both later phases build on.

sutra — phase 1 of the re-sequenced finale✓ shipped · ▶ do next · ◐ build-clean · ○ todo · → drill in
✓S1 · S2 · R9.0 · R9.1 — memoryless producer · kolu's fold · in-process awareness · the resolvershipped #1626 #1603 · → awareness
▶SUBSTRATE · extract @kolu/sutra — connectSutra(host) · sealed single-host dispatch · private endpoints · dial/reconnect/adoption · bare ptyId (no composite key, no HostLocation threading)the lifecycle prep · do next
○kolu-server re-homed onto sutra — local-preserving; the seam-hunt is now a compile errorthis PR
○KOLU-TUI · the second consumer, remote-first — embeds sutra, dials one host, proves it graduated→ kolu-tui
○F-REMOTE · the full web tile on the proven substrate + the host-selector dropdown→ finale
  • Needs: R9.1 (the resolver, shipped). Blocks: kolu-tui and the full client both embed the result.
  • Done: @kolu/sutra exists with the sealed single-host TerminalSubstrate facade + connectSutra(host); TerminalEndpoint / resolveTerminalEndpoint / localTerminalEndpoint / the nine *Local* helpers are package-private (importing one outside the package fails to compile); kolu-server drives every terminal op through the facade with a bare ptyId; HostLocation no longer threads through per-terminal ops (it lives on the connection); all existing terminal e2e + sleepWake.test.ts + boot-adoption tests pass unchanged. No user-facing change.

See also

  • kolu-tui — the second consumer that proves sutra: kolu’s Dock + terminal, one host at a time.
  • The finale — the re-sequenced phase plan of record (sutra → kolu-tui → full-client F-REMOTE + host dropdown → R10).
  • Awareness — observe vs. remember — the memoryless producer / kolu-owns-memory line sutra honors.
  • Electricity — the receptacle bar this clears, and the table it joins.

Referenced by

  • Awareness — the producer observes, kolu remembers
